opp.name: Teads [P2P] March2026
opp.next_step: complete RFP Why not Zycus The don’t use MS Teams. They use Google chat which according to Bikash is not on the roadmap. This takes away one of our biggest advantages. They have 1,800 users of eProc today and are looking to grow this. They already use Basware and Coupa. Their response: The primary driver for this RFP is global consolidation following the merger. We want to explore the latest market developments and AI tools to find an agile, global solution. Current challenges with existing platforms include managing a long tail of countries, maintaining local compliance while ensuring an easy global process, global budget visibility, supplier pushback, and ease of configurations. Which we do well but will need to reevaluate whether we stay in the deal or not after next discussion with prospect and have them explain why Zycus.
opp.ais_why:
• Strong buyer-stated AI signal in the next-step note, quoting the Teads response directly: "explore the latest market developments and AI tools to find an agile, global solution". That is verbatim buyer voice on AI, in the context of a post-Outbrain-merger consolidation where they explicitly want to leverage AI as a market trend in vendor selection.
• Working against that is a key fit gap: "They don't use MS Teams. They use Google chat which according to Bikash is not on the roadmap". The internal Zycus note adds "This takes away one of our biggest advantages". Teads already runs Basware and Coupa with 1,800 eProc users. The product scope is also narrower - Merlin and Merlin Intake only, no Agentic AI or ANA - and no specific Merlin or agentic ask has been recorded beyond the general 'AI tools' line.
• Avoma was fully exhausted - zero calls across opportunity, account, teads.tv, teads.com and outbrain.com. The verbatim AI-tools quote is real buyer evidence, but the Teams/Intake fit gap and the internal note about reevaluating whether to stay in the deal cap the upside. This is a Curious deal that could move either way depending on the next conversation.

items.quote: No EB identified; no CFO or budget authority engaged on this opp.
No champion role identified; Tamar Avni (SVP AI) is the sole contact with no evidence of advocacy.
RFP due date, shortlist date, and decision timeline unknown.
Qualitative pain stated but no quantified savings, ROI, or cost impact.
Five vendors named but no recent intel on demos, pricing, or finalist status.
items.source: Competitor field (last updated 05 Jun, no recent tasks/calls)

items.quote: Zero Avoma calls discovered via opp, account, or attendee email discovery; no transcript or notes available.
items.source: Avoma discovery (opp + account + attendee pulls returned zero matched calls)

items.quote: Only 1 buyer-side contact role (Tamar Avni); no procurement lead, CFO, or decision-maker engaged.

sources: NextStep field (last modified 05 Jun 2026)
Buyer response (blue text): post-merger consolidation pain
metrics.narrative: The buyer articulated qualitative pain in the Next Step field (quoted blue text, captured ~early Jun 2026): managing a long tail of countries post-merger, maintaining local compliance while ensuring an easy global process, global budget visibility, supplier pushback, and ease of configurations. These are process/compliance pain points but no quantified impact (cost, cycle time, error rate, savings target) has been stated. The 1,800 current eProcurement users provides scale context but no dollar value or ROI expectation. Without a quantified business case the deal lacks a financial anchor for the Economic Buyer conversation. The next move is to convert these qualitative pains into a quantified savings or efficiency model during the RFP response or a follow-on value workshop.

sources: OpportunityContactRole (Tamar Avni, no evidence of advocacy)
Task 09 Jun (Anush Jeunet mentioned, role unclear)
NextStep (no champion attribution)
champion.narrative: No champion is identified. The single contact role is Tamar Avni (SVP AI & Information System Engineering), a technical executive whose title suggests AI/systems focus, potentially aligned with the Merlin value prop, but there is no evidence of advocacy, internal selling, or access to the Economic Buyer or decision committee. The Next Step note is rep-authored and does not quote Tamar or attribute the blue-text buyer response to her. No tasks, emails, or calls document her engagement level, introductions provided, or competitive intelligence shared. A recent task (09 Jun) mentions 'Anush Jeunet' (Director of Global Procurement) but she is not a contact role on this opp and her involvement is unconfirmed. With a five-vendor RFP, 1,800 users, and two incumbents, a champion who can navigate the internal politics and push Zycus over Basware/Coupa is critical, and we do not have one. The immediate move is to assess Tamar's willingness and ability to champion, and if she is not the right profile, identify and cultivate the procurement or finance leader who owns the business case.

sources: Competitor field (5 vendors)
NextStep (Basware/Coupa incumbents, Google Chat issue, internal doubt)
No recent competitive refresh (last modified 05 Jun)
competition.narrative: Five competitors are named in the Salesforce Competitor field (Basware, Coupa, GEP Worldwide, iValua, JAGGAER), all active in the RFP per the field population date and Next Step context (~early Jun). Basware and Coupa are incumbents ('They already use Basware and Coupa', Next Step), giving them deployment inertia, user familiarity, and switching-cost leverage. GEP, iValua, and JAGGAER are peer suite vendors, all credible in large enterprise P2P RFPs. No recent competitive intelligence (win themes, pricing, buyer sentiment per vendor) exists; the competitor list is a raw enumeration with no scored threat level or wedge strategy. The internal 'Why not Zycus' note flags a critical weakness: Teads uses Google Chat, not MS Teams, and Google Chat integration is not on Zycus's roadmap per Bikash, eliminating a core Zycus differentiator (collaboration-layer advantage). This is a structural disadvantage versus competitors who may support Google Workspace. The note also records internal doubt: 'will need to reevaluate whether we stay in the deal... and have them explain why Zycus', signaling the rep is questioning fit and winability. No recent calls, tasks, or Next Step updates provide competitive updates (e.g., vendor demos completed, finalist shortlist, pricing comparisons). The next move is to run a competitive strategy session to assess whether the Google Chat gap and incumbent advantage are surmountable, and if so, to build a wedge around Merlin AI, global compliance, and post-merger consolidation agility where Basware/Coupa may be weak.

sources: NextStep field (buyer response, blue text)
Account contact (Anush Jeunet, Director Global Procurement, not confirmed on opp)
Products field (1,800 users mentioned in Next Step)
identify_pain.narrative: The buyer's primary pain is post-merger operational complexity: managing procurement across a long tail of countries with disparate compliance requirements, lack of global budget visibility, supplier pushback (adoption/usability issues), and configuration rigidity in their current Basware/Coupa stack (Next Step, blue text, ~early Jun). The pain owner is not named but is inferred to be the Global Procurement function (an account contact, Anush Jeunet, holds the title Director of Global Procurement, though not confirmed as engaged in this opp). The pain is confirmed and operationally specific, but not financially quantified (no stated cost of compliance failures, budget-variance impact, or supplier-friction cost). The 1,800 current eProcurement users indicates scale, and the merger context creates urgency, but the compelling event date and consequence ('what if this is not done') are not stated. The next move is to anchor the pain to a quantified impact and a date-driven event (e.g., fiscal-year budget lock, merger integration deadline).

sources: Products field (7 modules, iSaaS)
No field or task evidence of paper-process discussion
paper_process.narrative: No contracting, legal, procurement, or InfoSec process is documented. We do not know if Teads requires an MSA, SOW, or order-form-only flow, whether InfoSec review is required (likely given the enterprise scale and AI components), or if an SI or implementation partner is involved. The Products field includes iSaaS and seven modules (iSupplier, eProcurement, eInvoicing, Merlin, iRisk, Merlin Intake), implying a complex integration and likely a formal SOW, but no evidence confirms this. The current contract expiration date for the incumbents (Basware, Coupa) is unknown, so we cannot anchor to a forcing function. The next step is to ask the buyer about their procurement/legal process, typical contract vehicle, InfoSec requirements, and whether the RFP includes an implementation/services component.

sources: OpportunityContactRole query (1 contact: Tamar Avni, technical role)
Account contacts (CFOs and VP FP&A unconfirmed for this opp)
NextStep field (no EB named)
economic_buyer.narrative: No Economic Buyer is identified in the contact roles, and no evidence from calls, tasks, or Next Step names who controls budget or pricing authority. The single contact role is Tamar Avni (SVP AI & Information System Engineering), a technical/engineering leader unlikely to own a $397k P2P procurement budget. Account-level contacts include two CFOs (Armando Gil, North America; Gabriela Lugo, LATAM) and a VP FP&A (Mathew Reynders), but none are linked to this opportunity or confirmed as engaged. The Next Step mentions the buyer's 'response' generically with no attributed speaker. With 188 days to close and a five-vendor RFP, failing to map and engage the Economic Buyer in Formal Evaluation is a critical gap. The immediate move is to ask Tamar Avni (or the procurement lead if discovered) who approves the P2P budget and request an introduction.

sources: NextStep field (RFP mentioned, no timeline)
Competitor field (5 vendors)
No field-history or task evidence of process discussion
decision_process.narrative: No decision process is documented. We do not know the RFP timeline (shortlist date, demo rounds, vendor selection gate, contract start), the evaluation committee composition, the approval chain, or who makes the final call. The Next Step says 'complete RFP' but does not state when responses are due, when shortlisting occurs, or the decision date. With five named competitors (Basware, Coupa, GEP, iValua, JAGGAER) and two incumbents (Basware, Coupa) already deployed, a structured multi-round process is likely, but we have no visibility. The 31 Dec 2026 close date is 188 days out, suggesting time exists, but without milestones we cannot pace the deal or forecast accurately. The immediate move is to ask the buyer (Tamar Avni or the procurement lead) for the RFP timeline, shortlist and selection dates, and the decision-maker roster.

sources: NextStep field (buyer response, blue text, ~early Jun)
Internal note (Google Chat vs MS Teams incompatibility)
decision_criteria.narrative: The buyer stated their evaluation criteria in the Next Step blue-text response (captured ~early Jun): global consolidation capability post-merger, latest AI tools, agility, long-tail country coverage with local compliance, easy global process, global budget visibility, supplier adoption (pushback mitigation), and ease of configuration. These are use-case-driven criteria directly relevant to Zycus iSupplier/eProcurement/Merlin. However, the criteria are qualitative and drawn from a single summary statement, not from a formal RFP scorecard, use-case workshop, or structured demo debrief. We do not know weighting, scoring methodology, or whether AI/Merlin is a must-have or nice-to-have. The internal 'Why not Zycus' note flags Google Chat incompatibility as a disqualifier for the MS Teams advantage, which may be a hidden criterion. The next step is to request the formal RFP evaluation matrix and confirm how AI/Merlin and collaboration-tool integrations are weighted.

cro_panel.intro: One read per score, in language a CRO/CO can act on. We're behind on this one — the reads below say why and what would change it.

blocks.read: We're well behind — this one is cold.

ai_fit_signal.summary: AIS_Score__c, AIS_Status__c, AIS_Why__c are all null in Salesforce. Buyer explicitly stated 'latest AI tools' as an RFP driver (Next Step, blue text), and Merlin + Merlin Intake are in Products scope, suggesting AI Curious or AI Hungry. However, no call, demo, or workshop evidence exists to confirm depth of AI appetite or whether Merlin has been positioned. The SVP AI contact (Tamar Avni) implies organizational AI focus, but her engagement on this deal is unproven. Inferred tier: AI Curious (stated interest, no validated excitement). Risk: AI interest may be checkbox-level ('nice to have') rather than decision-driver, and no evidence shows Zycus has made Merlin the centerpiece versus GEP or other AI-claiming competitors.

north_star_verdict.headline: 17 days of buyer silence post-RFP submission with no confirmation received. Rep openly questions staying in the deal due to Google Chat incompatibility, two entrenched incumbents across 1,800 users, five-vendor RFP, and zero multi-threading or champion mapped.

best_practice_check.summary: Severe single-threading (1 contact), no EB/Champion, Google Chat structural disadvantage, and 17-day buyer silence in a 5-vendor RFP create existential risk. Immediate moves: re-engage Tamar Avni to confirm RFP status and request introductions to procurement lead and CFO; assess Google Chat disqualification impact with product/exec before investing further; multi-thread aggressively if staying in.

competitive_position.summary: Teads is running a five-vendor RFP (Basware, Coupa, GEP, iValua, JAGGAER) with two incumbents (Basware, Coupa) already deployed to 1,800 eProcurement users. Basware and Coupa are the highest current threats due to deployment inertia, user training investment, and switching cost; the buyer must justify displacement, not just feature parity. GEP is the likely peer suite threat (large enterprise, P2P+AI, global compliance strength). Zycus faces a structural disadvantage: the buyer uses Google Chat, and Zycus does not support it (MS Teams-only per Bikash, not on roadmap), eliminating a core collaboration differentiator. Internal rep note (05 Jun) expresses doubt about staying in the deal and asks the buyer to 'explain why Zycus', signaling weak positioning. No recent competitive intel (demos, pricing, finalist status) exists. Recommended wedge: Merlin AI for post-merger global consolidation, long-tail country compliance automation, and budget visibility if Basware/Coupa cannot match; but viability depends on whether the Google Chat gap is disqualifying.

competitors.name: Basware
competitors.quote: They already use Basware and Coupa.
competitors.how_we_win: Prove Merlin AI and global compliance agility exceed Basware's current capability for post-merger long-tail country management; quantify switching ROI to overcome inertia.

competitors.name: Coupa
competitors.quote: They already use Basware and Coupa.
competitors.how_we_win: Emphasize Merlin AI differentiation and ease of configuration versus Coupa's complexity; address supplier pushback with iSupplier UX advantage.

competitors.name: GEP Worldwide
competitors.how_we_win: Benchmark Merlin AI negotiation and intake agents against GEP Smart; highlight Zycus post-merger consolidation references if GEP lacks them.

competitors.name: iValua
competitors.how_we_win: Position Merlin AI and faster deployment versus iValua's heavyweight suite; stress agility and ease of configuration per buyer criteria.

competitors.name: JAGGAER
competitors.how_we_win: Lead with Merlin AI and global compliance depth; compare Zycus's post-merger reference portfolio against JAGGAER's if weaker.

ai_positioning_strength.summary: Products field includes Merlin and Merlin Intake, and buyer stated 'latest AI tools' as a priority (Next Step, blue text, ~early Jun). AIS fields are unpopulated so no AI excitement score exists, but the buyer's explicit RFP criterion ('AI tools') and the SVP AI contact (Tamar Avni) suggest AI Curious or AI Hungry. However, no demo, workshop, or call has validated whether Merlin's negotiation/intake agents resonate or whether the buyer sees AI as must-have vs nice-to-have. The internal note flags doubt about staying in the deal, with no mention of Merlin as a wedge, suggesting AI positioning has not been activated as the primary differentiator. Under-positioned given the buyer's stated interest and the competitive field (GEP has Smart AI, others may claim AI parity).

Multi-module scope (7 products) matches the post-merger complexity, but no demo, workshop, or use-case validation has occurred to confirm fit depth.

• Working against that is a key fit gap: "They don't use MS Teams. They use Google chat which according to Bikash is not on the roadmap". The internal Zycus note adds "This takes away one of our biggest advantages". Teads already runs Basware and Coupa with 1,800 eProc users. The product scope is also narrower - Merlin and Merlin Intake only, no Agentic AI or ANA - and no specific Merlin or agentic ask has been recorded beyond the general 'AI tools' line.
• Avoma was fully exhausted - zero calls across opportunity, account, teads.tv, teads.com and outbrain.com. The verbatim AI-tools quote is real buyer evidence, but the Teams/Intake fit gap and the internal note about reevaluating whether to stay in the deal cap the upside. This is a Curious deal that could move either way depending on the next conversation.

deltas.subject: Google Chat incompatibility (MS Teams not supported per Bikash, not on roadmap) eliminates a core Zycus differentiator and may be disqualifying per internal note 05 Jun. Before investing further in RFP response, escalate to product leadership and the deal owner's manager to confirm whether a workaround exists or whether Zycus should exit the deal. If staying in, the wedge must shift entirely to Merlin AI post-merger consolidation value where Basware/Coupa cannot match.

deltas.to: No buyer engagement in 17 days (last activity 09 Jun) and RFP completion milestone unmet suggests the buyer is either evaluating other vendors first, deprioriti
deltas.subject: No buyer engagement in 17 days (last activity 09 Jun) and RFP completion milestone unmet suggests the buyer is either evaluating other vendors first, deprioritizing Zycus, or the internal rep doubt (05 Jun note) has stalled outreach. Re-engage Tamar Avni within 3 days with a value-add touch (e.g., post-merger compliance insight, Merlin demo offer) to confirm RFP timeline and Zycus's standing.
scope

value.requirement: Complete and submit the RFP response.

packets.subject: Buyer:Buyer to explain why Zycus should remain in the RFP given Google Chat incompatibility.

value.name: Tamar Avni
value.risk: Single-threaded; no evidence of advocacy or access to EB/DM; technical role may not drive commercial decision.
value.title: SVP AI & Information System Engineering

value.name: Anush Jeunet
value.risk: Mentioned in task 09 Jun but not a contact role on opp; likely procurement lead but engagement unconfirmed.
value.title: Director of Global Procurement

value.name: Armando Gil
value.risk: Account contact, not on opp; potential Economic Buyer but no confirmed involvement.
value.title: CFO, North America

value.name: Gabriela Lugo
value.risk: Account contact, not on opp; potential Economic Buyer for LATAM scope but no confirmed involvement.
value.title: CFO Latin America

value.name: Mathew Reynders
value.risk: Account contact, not on opp; may influence budget approval but engagement unconfirmed.
value.title: Vice President, Financial Planning & Analysis

value.name: Basware
value.quote: They already use Basware and Coupa.
value.how_we_win: Prove Merlin AI and global compliance agility exceed Basware's current capability for post-merger long-tail country management; quantify switching ROI to overcome inertia.

value.name: Coupa
value.quote: They already use Basware and Coupa.
value.how_we_win: Emphasize Merlin AI differentiation and ease of configuration versus Coupa's complexity; address supplier pushback with iSupplier UX advantage.

value.name: GEP Worldwide
value.how_we_win: Benchmark Merlin AI negotiation and intake agents against GEP Smart; highlight Zycus post-merger consolidation references if GEP lacks them.

value.name: iValua
value.how_we_win: Position Merlin AI and faster deployment versus iValua's heavyweight suite; stress agility and ease of configuration per buyer criteria.

value.name: JAGGAER
value.how_we_win: Lead with Merlin AI and global compliance depth; compare Zycus's post-merger reference portfolio against JAGGAER's if weaker.

value.detail: Google Chat incompatibility (MS Teams not supported, not on Zycus roadmap per Bikash) eliminates collaboration-layer differentiator and may disqualify Zycus per internal assessment.

value.detail: Two incumbents (Basware, Coupa) already deployed to 1,800 users create high switching cost, user training burden, and deployment inertia; Zycus must prove ROI exceeds switching pain.

packets.subject: Two incumbents (Basware, Coupa) already deployed to 1,800 users create high switching cost, user training burden, and deployment inertia; Zycus must prove ROI exceeds switching pain.

value.detail: Internal rep doubt recorded: 'will need to reevaluate whether we stay in the deal... and have them explain why Zycus' signals weak seller conviction and possible early exit consideration.

value.detail: Single-threaded to the relevant stakeholder (technical role) with no access to procurement lead, CFO, or decision-maker in a five-vendor RFP means zero political capital or champion to counter incumbents.

packets.subject: Single-threaded to the relevant stakeholder (technical role) with no access to procurement lead, CFO, or decision-maker in a five-vendor RFP means zero political capital or champion to counter incumbents.

value.detail: Five active competitors (Basware, Coupa, GEP, iValua, JAGGAER) with no recent competitive intel (demos, pricing, finalist status) means blind competitive positioning and high risk of being outflanked or priced out.

packets.subject: Five active competitors (Basware, Coupa, GEP, iValua, JAGGAER) with no recent competitive intel (demos, pricing, finalist status) means blind competitive positioning and high risk of being outflanked or priced out.

value.flag: Single contact role (Tamar Avni, SVP AI) in a five-vendor enterprise RFP with 1,800 users and two incumbents is extreme risk; no access to procurement, finance, or decision-maker means zero visibility into scoring, internal champion, or finalist probability. Add buyer-side contact roles (procurement lead, CFO, decision-maker) to the opportunity and secure introductions within 7 days.

gaps: Zero Avoma calls discovered via opp (006P700000UuvSA), account (0010O00002AWKVLQA5), or attendee email (tamar.avni@teads.com, anush.jeunet@teads.com, and other account contacts) discovery pulls. No transcript, notes, or competitive intel available from buyer conversations.
No field-history stage progression (deal created 06 Mar in Formal Evaluation, no movement since).
No Tasks or Events document RFP response activity, demo requests, or buyer deliverables (only generic 09 Jun task mentioning Anush Jeunet).
AIS fields (AIS_Score__c, AIS_Status__c, AIS_Why__c) all null; no AI excitement scoring available.
MEDDPICC synthesis-map fields (Q3 SOQL) returned INVALID_FIELD errors or null; no Salesforce-stored MEDDPICC data beyond contact roles.
No Economic Buyer, Decision Maker, or Champion contact roles populated.
No submission-date fields (Qualified_Submission_Date__c, Formal_Eval_Submission_Date__c, Shortlisted_Submission_Date__c) populated; time-in-stage cannot be computed from submission dates.
Next_Step_History__c field not readable (INVALID_FIELD or null); only current Next_Step available.

They have 1,800 users of eProc today and are looking to grow this. They already use Basware and Coupa.Their response:The primary driver for this RFP is global consolidation following the merger. We want to explore the latest market developments and AI tools to find an agile, global solution. Current challenges with existing platforms include managing a long tail of countries, maintaining local compliance while ensuring an easy global process, global budget visibility, supplier pushback, and ease of configurations.\xa0Which we do well but will need to reevaluate whether we stay in the deal or not after next discussion with prospect and have them explain why Zycus.' diverges from Salesforce '

complete RFP


Why not Zycus

The don’t use MS Teams. They use Google chat which according to Bikash is not on the roadmap. This takes away one of our biggest advantages. They have 1,800 users of eProc today and are looking to grow this. They already use Basware and Coupa.


Their response:

The primary driver for this RFP is global consolidation following the merger. We want to explore the latest market developments and AI tools to find an agile, global solution. Current challenges with existing platforms include managing a long tail of countries, maintaining local compliance while ensuring an easy global process, global budget visibility, supplier pushback, and ease of configurations.

\xa0

Which we do well but will need to reevaluate whether we stay in the deal or not after next discussion with prospect and have them explain why Zycus.

evidence_coverage.salesforce_window: Q1: standard fields (Id, Name, Account, Owner, Stage, Amount, CloseDate, etc.); Q2: AIS, Products, submission dates, Next_Step_History__c; Q3: MEDDPICC/competition synthesis fields (all returned INVALID_FIELD or null); field history 365d, tasks/events 90d, contact roles, line items.
